findings.description: Oh splendid — the full card number AND the CVV are written in plaintext to the application log, a PCI-DSS bonfire that also illegally persists the CVV after authorization.

Transaction IDs are minted from Python's non-cryptographic, per-process-randomized hash() of card digits modulo 10**10, so they are unstable across restarts and collision-prone — truly inspired idempotency.
charge_order sets status='paid' and tx_id but never writes paid_at, yet monthly_revenue_report filters strictly on paid_at, so every order this code charges becomes invisible to the revenue report.

Money is accumulated and taxed as binary floats and only converted to integer cents at the very end, guaranteeing rounding drift on real orders — because who needs accurate totals in a payments system.
If gateway.charge succeeds but the subsequent UPDATE/commit raises, the customer is charged while the order stays unpaid with no compensating refund or idempotency key — money in, record out.
discount_pct is never validated or clamped, so a discount above 100 yields negative line totals (and a negative grand total) while a negative discount silently inflates the charge.

monthly_revenue_report recomputes revenue from current order_items prices with no discount applied, so the reported figure diverges from what was actually charged whenever a discount or price change exists.
Classic N+1: a separate order_items query fires inside the per-order loop instead of a single JOIN/GROUP BY, so the report scales beautifully... linearly with order count in round trips.
The cvv parameter is threaded through charge_order only to be logged and never used for an actual authorization decision, so its sole function is to leak sensitive data into logs.
The gateway's only guard is amount_cents <= 0, which would swallow a negative total produced by a >100% discount as a generic 'non-positive amount' instead of surfacing the upstream pricing bug.
The module cheerfully mixes dollars (compute_order_total) and cents (revenue_cents/amount_cents) with no unit-typed wrapper, an inconsistency begging a caller to multiply by 100 one too many times.
